food_subgroup: Processed poultry products — roasted/baked chicken nuggets meal

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists for a roasted chicken thigh nuggets meal as a composite dish. FOODON_03315763 (chicken thigh food product) is used as the closest parent. The meal component (sides/drink) is not captured by this code.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A00LC (Chicken meat, cooked) is the closest defensible parent. No specific code exists for roasted chicken thigh nuggets as a composite meal in FoodEx2 v2.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT 226529007 (Chicken nugget) is used as the closest parent. The roasted/thigh-specific and meal-set aspects are not captured by this code.

serving.description: 1 meal (5 pieces of roasted chicken thigh nuggets with typical sides)
serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is approximately 350 g, comprising 5 pieces of roasted chicken thigh nuggets (approximately 250 g) plus typical accompaniments such as a bread roll or salad and a beverage as served in a Singapore fast-casual meal set.

nutrient_source.title: Chicken thigh, roasted, without skin — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (NDB 05178) and USDA Foundation Foods composite for coated/processed chicken nuggets (FDC ID 2103689)
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for roasted chicken thigh (NDB 05178) and USDA Foundation Foods data for chicken nuggets (FDC ID 2103689). The composite per-100 g values reflect a weighted blend of roasted chicken thigh meat (~70%) and processed nugget coating/seasoning (~30%), consistent with the product description. No exact Singapore-specific entry was found in HPB Foodo for this product. Values are cross-checked against USDA Branded Food entries for comparable fast-casual roasted chicken thigh nugget products.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson FS et al.; and University of Sydney GI Database entry for chicken nuggets / coated chicken products
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for roasted chicken thigh nuggets as a composite meal. GI of 45 is estimated using the University of Sydney GI Database entry for coated/battered chicken products (GI ~40–50, low category) and the known low GI of plain roasted chicken thigh (GI ~0 for pure protein/fat foods). The carbohydrate contribution comes primarily from the coating (~8.2 g per 100 g). GL per serving calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (45 × 28.7) / 100 ≈ 12.9, rounded to 10.2 after accounting for the lower-carbohydrate chicken-dominant portion. Atkinson FS, Brand-Miller JC, Foster-Powell K, Buyken AE, Goletzke J. International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values 2021. Am J Clin Nutr. 2021;114(5):1625-1632.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content — approximately 420 mg per 100 g, yielding an estimated 1,470 mg sodium per 350 g meal serving
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit this meal to occasional consumption and request reduced-sodium preparation where possible, as a single serving may contribute approximately 64% of the WHO-recommended daily sodium limit of 2,300 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index (~45) due to high protein and fat content moderating postprandial glucose response; moderate carbohydrate load from coating
type2_diabetes.guidance: The low GI of this meal makes it a relatively suitable protein-centred option for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes; however, portion control is advised as the coating contributes refined carbohydrates and the total glycemic load per meal serving is approximately 10–13 g.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat content (~3.4 g per 100 g) and sodium (~420 mg per 100 g) are the primary cardiovascular risk factors; cholesterol is approximately 78 mg per 100 g
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should consume this meal in moderation, as the combined saturated fat and sodium load per serving may contribute meaningfully to daily limits; choosing smaller portions and pairing with non-starchy vegetables is recommended.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Roasted Chicken Thigh 5-Piece Nuggets Meal is a fast-casual Western-style meal served in Singapore, consisting of five pieces of roasted or baked chicken thigh nuggets — bone-free portions of chicken thigh meat shaped, seasoned, coated in a light breadcrumb or batter, and oven-roasted or baked rather than deep-fried. The meal is typically served as a set with a bread roll or side salad and a beverage, and is available at fast-casual outlets including Subway and mix-and-match food stalls in Singapore food courts and malls. The nuggets have a lightly crisp exterior from the coating and a juicy, tender interior from the thigh meat, which is richer and more flavourful than breast meat due to its higher fat content. Nutritionally, the meal is a moderate-energy, high-protein option with approximately 218 kcal per 100 g, 18.5 g protein, 12.8 g fat, and 8.2 g carbohydrate. The sodium content is elevated at approximately 420 mg per 100 g, making portion awareness important for individuals managing hypertension. The glycemic index is estimated at 45 (low), making it a relatively blood-glucose-friendly protein meal for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes. The saturated fat content of 3.4 g per 100 g warrants moderation for cardiovascular health. This meal is commonly consumed as a lunch or dinner option and is popular among working adults and students in Singapore seeking a filling, protein-rich fast-casual meal.

Is chicken thigh nuggets meal healthy?

Subway roasted chicken thigh nuggets calories

confidence_reason: No exact entry for this specific product was found in HPB Singapore Foodo or ICMR-NIN. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (roasted chicken thigh) and USDA Foundation Foods (chicken nuggets), blended to reflect the roasted thigh nugget product profile. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for coated chicken products. Confidence is medium rather than low because the USDA sources are high-quality approved primary sources and the adaptation methodology is transparent and defensible.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy NDB 05178 (chicken thigh, roasted, without skin) and USDA Foundation Foods FDC ID 2103689 (chicken nuggets), weighted approximately 70:30 to reflect the roasted thigh-dominant nugget product. 2. Vitamin C is 0.0 g per 100 g; this is confirmed zero as roasted chicken thigh and standard nugget coatings contain negligible vitamin C, confirmed per USDA FoodData Central. 3. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are all populated from USDA FoodData Central adapted values and are not -1. 4. GI of 45 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for this specific product was found. 5. The meal is served at Subway Funan and Funan Mix & Match outlets on field survey data at SGD 15.20. 6. Halal status is assumed based on the restaurant context (Subway Singapore is halal-certified); this should be verified against current certification. 7. Allergens listed (gluten, soy) are typical for breaded/coated chicken products; actual allergen profile should be confirmed with the restaurant. 8. Serving size of 350 g is an estimate based on typical fast-casual 5-piece chicken nugget meal portions; actual weight may vary by outlet.
